items.q: Does the state symbol (l) mean dissolved in water?
items.correct: No — (l) means a pure liquid such as molten or liquid substances; dissolved substances are shown as (aq).
items.misconception: (l) stands for 'in liquid', so it means the substance is dissolved in water.
items.random_wrong: (l) means the substance is a gas kept at low temperature.
items.why: The everyday word 'liquid' covers solutions too, so students miss the chemical distinction between a pure liquid and an aqueous solution.

items.q: Can you change the small subscript numbers in formulae to balance an equation?
items.correct: No — subscripts define the identity of each compound; you balance only by writing numbers in front of whole formulae.
items.misconception: Writing MgO₂ instead of MgO is an acceptable way to balance the oxygen atoms.
items.random_wrong: You balance equations by deleting products you do not want.
items.why: Subscripts and coefficients both look like 'numbers to adjust'; changing a subscript silently changes the substance itself.

items.q: In Ca(NO₃)₂, does the 2 outside the bracket multiply only the oxygen?
items.correct: No — it multiplies everything inside the bracket, giving two nitrogen atoms and six oxygen atoms.
items.misconception: The 2 applies only to the last atom written, the oxygen.
items.random_wrong: The 2 means there are two calcium atoms.
items.why: Students read formulae left to right like text and attach trailing numbers to the nearest symbol rather than the whole bracket.

items.q: Which is the correctly balanced equation for magnesium burning in oxygen?
items.why: Correct answer: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. “Mg + O₂ → MgO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The state symbol for a substance dissolved in water is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: (aq). “(l)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many atoms in total are in one molecule of sulfuric acid, H₂SO₄?
items.why: The real figure is 7 atoms. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
